elements.sent-2411-001: Qian Du is with the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Mississippi State University, Starkville, MS 39762 USA.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recently Transformer-based hyperspectral image (HSI) change detection methods have shown remarkable performance.
elements.sent-2411-003: Nevertheless, existing attention mechanisms in Transformers have limitations in local feature representation.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this issue, we propose Global and Local Attention-based Transformer (GLAFormer), which incorporates a global and local attention module (GLAM) to combine high-frequency and low-frequency signals.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we introduce a cross-gating mechanism, called cross-gated feed-forward network (CGFN), to emphasize salient features and suppress noise interference.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, the GLAM splits attention heads into global and local attention components to capture comprehensive spatial-spectral features.
elements.sent-2411-007: The global attention component employs global attention on downsampled feature maps to capture low-frequency information, while the local attention component focuses on high-frequency details using non-overlapping window-based local attention.
elements.sent-2411-008: The CGFN enhances the feature representation via convolutions and cross-gating mechanism in parallel paths.
elements.sent-2411-009: The proposed GLAFormer is evaluated on three HSI datasets.
elements.sent-2411-010: The results demonstrate its superiority over state-of-the-art HSI change detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-011: The source code of GLAFormer is available at \url{https://github.com/summitgao/GLAFormer .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The exceptional spectral resolution of HSIs facilitates accurate detection of changes in ground objects \cite{ding22grsl .}
elements.sent-2411-013: As such, HSI change detection has been widely applied in various domains, including damage assessment \cite{entcheva04ijrs , land cover analysis \cite{8738052 and urban expansion monitoring\cite{9773336 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Traditional methods in HSI change detection primarily employed techniques, such as change vector analysis \cite{cva06tgrs and Tucker decomposition \cite{hou21jstars , to analyze the spectral changes in multi-temporal images.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Canty et al. \cite{niemeyer2003pixel were the first to introduce the Multivariate Alteration Detection (MAD) method, which is based on Canonical Correlation Analysis and is designed for unsupervised change detection in vegetation using multi-temporal hyperspectral images.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Later, Nielsen \cite{nielsen2007regularized enhanced this approach by developing the Iteratively Reweighted MAD (IR-MAD) algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, these methods encountered limitations in threshold selection and reduced robustness in complex scenarios \cite{hasanlou2018hyperspectral .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Recently, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) \cite{lecun1998gradient have been proven especially effective in extracting representative features from multi-temporal HSIs.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Saha et al. \cite{deepcva proposed a method called deep CVA by combining CNNs with change vector analysis (CVA).}
elements.sent-2411-020: More recently, Transformers, which rely entirely on self-attention, have gained popularity in computer vision tasks, such as image classification \cite{vit and object detection \cite{dert .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Transformers have the advantage of capturing global dependencies and exhibit better performance in handling long-range dependencies compared to CNNs.
elements.sent-2411-022: This potential has prompted researchers to explore the attention mechanisms for HSI change detection.
elements.sent-2411-023: Song et al. \cite{song2022csanet enhanced the feature representation of multi-temporal HSIs by introducing cross-temporal interaction symmetric attention.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Furthermore, Ding et al. \cite{ding22grsl introduced the Transformer encoder for HSI change detection, leveraging self-attention with a global receptive field to enhance the recognition of changes.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In \cite{wang2022vtc , a Transformer-based multi-scale feature fusion model was proposed for change detection.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics [width=6.8in]{img_frame.pdf} \caption{Overview of the proposed GLAFormer. In the GLAFormer block, high-frequency and low-frequency signals are fused through global and local attention module (GLAM). The ability to capture local context is enhanced through the cross-gated feed-forward network (CGFN). The final output is the Change Map, where white pixels represent the changed areas.} \label{img_frame}
elements.sent-2411-026: Although existing Transformer-based methods for HSI change detection have achieved promising performance, they still suffer from two limitations: 1) Insufficient local-level representations modeling.
elements.sent-2411-027: Transformers tend to pay more attention to global features.
elements.sent-2411-028: However, global and local features serve distinct roles in encoding HSI patterns.
elements.sent-2411-029: The emphasis on global features in Transformers leads to the loss of certain local features, which results in a degradation of change detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-030: Feed-Forward Network (FFN) is commonly used to process the output from the attention layer in Transformer, enabling non-linear feature transformation for the input of the subsequent attention layer.
elements.sent-2411-031: However, existing methods are limited in non-linear feature representation and are susceptible to noise interference.
elements.sent-2411-032: To overcome the above limitations, we propose a Global and Local Attention-based Transformer for HSI change detection, GLAFormer for short.
elements.sent-2411-033: Specifically, to enhance the local-level feature representations, we have designed a global and local attention module (GLAM) to encode both low-frequency and high-frequency signals.
elements.sent-2411-034: Furthermore, to augment the non-linear feature transformation, we propose the cross-gated feed-forward network (CGFN) to amplify the salient information while suppressing noise.
elements.sent-2411-035: Extensive experiments on three HSI change detection datasets demonstrate the superiority of our proposed GLAFormer.
elements.sent-2411-036: Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-037: We propose the GLAM as an enhancement to the self-attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-038: This module combines high-frequency and low-frequency signals to achieve a more comprehensive spatial-spectral feature representation for change detection.
elements.sent-2411-039: We develop the CGFN to improve the non-linear feature transformation within Transformers.
elements.sent-2411-040: This network amplifies important information and mitigates noise interference.
elements.sent-2411-041: Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed GLAFormer outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-042: The codes will be released to the remote sensing community.
elements.sent-2411-043: The overall architecture of our proposed GLAFormer is shown in Fig. \ref{img_frame .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Two hyperspectral images ($T_1$ and $T_2$) captured at different times are passed to the GLAFormer.
elements.sent-2411-045: Firstly, two patches from the multi-temporal HSIs of the same geographical area are extracted.
elements.sent-2411-046: Then, both patches are fed into two parallel GLAFormer encoders to extract informative and robust features.
elements.sent-2411-047: Next, the learned features from the two paths are fused.
elements.sent-2411-048: Finally, the fused features are transformed by several convolutional and fully connected layers for change detection.
elements.sent-2411-049: As shown in the right part of Fig. \ref{img_frame , the GLAFormer block consists of two key modules: GLAM and CGFN, which are detailed as below.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics [width=6.5in]{img_attn.pdf} \caption{Illustration of global and local attention module (GLAM). The module consists of two branches: global attention and local attention. The global attention branch captures the long-range dependencies of the input, while the local attention branch computes the detailed local feature dependency. Then, the global and local features are fused by concatenation.} \label{img_HiLo}
elements.sent-2411-050: As depicted in Fig. \ref{img_HiLo , the GLAM consists of two branches: global attention and local attention.}
elements.sent-2411-051: The global attention captures the global dependencies of the input, while the local attention branch computes the detailed local feature dependency.
elements.sent-2411-052: The global and local features are fused by concatenation.. Local attention.
elements.sent-2411-053: The local attention branch encodes high-frequency features via local window self-attention, which applies self-attention mechanism to local windows of feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-054: As shown in Fig \ref{img_HiLo , a local window refers to a $3\times3$ region in the feature maps.}
elements.sent-2411-055: These local windows are evenly partitioned in a non-overlapping manner.
elements.sent-2411-056: The feature within each window is of size $s \times s \times d$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Here, $s$ is the size of the window, and $d$ is the feature dimension.
elements.sent-2411-058: The feature within each window is reshaped into $\mathbf{X _{\text{l \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times s^2}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Next, a $1\times1$ convolution is applied to enhance the input and obtain the query $\mathbf{Q _{\text{l }$, key $\mathbf{K _{\text{l }$, and value $\mathbf{V _{\text{l }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The local window attention is defined as: \begin{equation}\hat{\mathbf{X}}_{\text{l}} = \mathbf{V}_{\text{l}} \cdot \text{Softmax}\left(\frac{\mathbf{K}_{\text{l}}^T \mathbf{Q}_{\text{l}}}{\sqrt{D}}\right),\end{equation} where $\hat{\mathbf{X _{\text{l }$ is the output feature from the local attention branch.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The global attention branch captures low-frequency features by applying the attention mechanism over pooled feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-062: As illustrated in Fig. \ref{img_HiLo , the input feature is evenly partitioned into $3\times3$ windows in a non-overlapping manner.}
elements.sent-2411-063: To effectively capture global information, average pooling is employed on each window to obtain the average-pooled feature map $\mathbf{Z \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times 9}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Next, $\mathbf{Z}$ is transformed to key $\mathbf{K _{\text{g \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times 9}$ and value $\mathbf{V _{\text{g \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times 9}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: To ensure complete and unchanged information access, the global attention uses queries $\mathbf{Q _{\text{l \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times 81}$ from the original feature map.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: This approach is consistent with that of local attention.
elements.sent-2411-067: To generate the output features $\hat{\mathbf{Z _{\text{g }$, the standard self-attention is applied on $\mathbf{Q _{\text{l }$, $\mathbf{K _{\text{g }$, and $\mathbf{V _{\text{g }$: \begin{equation}\hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{\text{g}} = \mathbf{V}_{\text{g}} \cdot \text{Softmax}\left(\frac{\mathbf{K}_{\text{g}}^T \mathbf{Q}_{\text{l}}}{\sqrt{D}}\right).\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The input features are evenly split along the channel dimension before entering the global and local attention branches, reducing complexity and boosting GPU throughput.
elements.sent-2411-070: This splitting also decomposes the learnable parameters into smaller matrices, reducing the model's parameter count.
elements.sent-2411-071: These two sets of features are separately fed into the local and global attention branches, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-072: To produce the output of GLAM, the output features $\hat{\mathbf{X _{\text{l }$ from the local attention branch and the output features $\hat{\mathbf{Z _{\text{g }$ from the global attention branch are concatenated as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{F}_{\text{GLAM}} = \text{concat}[\hat{\mathbf{X}}_{\text{l}}; \hat{\mathbf{Z}}_{\text{g}}].\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: To enhance the non-linear feature transformation in Transformers, the CGFN is proposed, which incorporates the gating mechanism and multi-scale convolution into the existing feed-forward network.
elements.sent-2411-074: As shown in Fig. \ref{img_dgfn , the proposed CGFN consists of two parallel paths.}
elements.sent-2411-075: In each path, depth-wise convolutions with different sizes of kernels are employed to enhance the multi-scale feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-076: Then, the gating mechanism is used to filter the less informative features in each path.
elements.sent-2411-077: The useful features passing through the gates are fused with the original features from another path.
elements.sent-2411-078: The fused features from the two paths are combined via element-wise summation.
elements.sent-2411-079: Given input $\mathbf{Y}$, the CGFN can be defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathrm{Gating}(\mathbf{Y}) = \phi\left(W_{3\times3} W_{1\times1}^0 \mathbf{Y}\right) \odot\left(W_{1\times1}^{2}W_{5\times5} W_{1 \times 1}^0 \mathbf{Y}\right) ~~~ +\phi\left(W_{5\times5} W_{1\times1}^0 \mathbf{Y} \right) \odot \left(W_{1\times1}^{3} W_{3 \times 3} W_{1 \times 1}^0 \mathbf{Y}\right), ~~ \end{split}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\hat{\mathbf{Y}} =W_{1\times1}^4 \mathrm{Gating}(\mathbf{Y})+\mathbf{Y},\end{equation} where $\hat{\mathbf{Y }$ denotes the output features, $\mathrm{Gating (\cdot)$ denotes the cross-gated mechanism, $\phi$ is the GELU activation function and $\odot$ represents the element-wise multiplication operation.}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation} Gating(Y) = \phi\left(W_{3\times3} W_{1\times1}^0 Y\right) \odot\left(W_{1\times1}^{2}W_{5\times5} W_{1 \times 1}^0 Y\right) ~~~ +\phi\left(W_{5\times5} W_{1\times1}^0 Y \right) \odot \left(W_{1\times1}^{3} W_{3 \times 3} W_{1 \times 1}^0 Y\right), ~~ \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-080: The gating mechanism and the multi-scale convolutions amplify important information and mitigate noise interference.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=3in]{img_river.pdf} \caption{Change detection results of different methods on River dataset. (a) IR-MAD. (b) SSA-SiamNet. (c) SSCNN-S. (d) CDFomer. (e) SSTFormer. (f) CSDBF. (g) Proposed GLAFormer. (h) Ground truth.} \label{fig_river}
elements.sent-2411-081: We evaluate the performance of our GLAFormer through extensive experiments on three widely recognized multi-temporal hyperspectral datasets.
elements.sent-2411-082: These datasets were sourced from the Hyperion sensor onboard the EO-1 satellite.
elements.sent-2411-083: Specifically, the first dataset, referred to as the River dataset \cite{river , comprises imagery of a river in Jiangsu Province, China.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The second dataset is the Farmland dataset \cite{farm , which covers a farmland area in Yancheng City, Jiangsu Province, China.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The third, known as the Hermiston dataset \cite{hasanlou2018hyperspectral , captures irrigated farmland in Hermiston City, Umatilla County, Oregon, USA.}
elements.sent-2411-086: To demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed GLAFormer, six state-of-the-art models are selected for comparison, i.e., IR-MAD \cite{nielsen2007regularized , SSA-SimaNet\cite{wang2021ssa , SSCNN-S \cite{zhan2021sscnn , CDFormer \cite{ding22grsl , SSTFormer \cite{wang2022SSTFormer , CSDBF \cite{wang2022csdbf and GTMSiam\cite{wang2023gtmsiam .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: GLAFormer is configured with 4 blocks and 8 attention heads across all three datasets.
elements.sent-2411-088: The comparative analysis is grounded in two primary metrics: overall accuracy (OA) and the Kappa coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-089: OA provides a general assessment of change detection performance in terms of overall correctness.
elements.sent-2411-090: Kappa is a more robust measure that takes into account the agreement between the observed classification and what would be expected by chance.
elements.sent-2411-091: All experiments are carried out by using the Pytorch framework.
elements.sent-2411-092: The training phase spanned over 100 epochs and was conducted on a single NVIDIA 4090 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-093: The Adam optimizer is utilized with a learning rate of 0.0006.
elements.sent-2411-094: The training batch size is set as 128.
elements.sent-2411-095: The input patch size for the proposed methods is $9\times9$, and the dimension of the embedded sequence is fixed at 256.
elements.sent-2411-096: For each of the three datasets, 3\% of the samples are selected for training, 2\% for validation, and the remaining samples are used for testing.
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Quantitative evaluation of different change detection methods on three datasets. The best performer is marked in \colorbox{Bg}{Blue} and the second best is underlined. The higher means better performance. } \label{table_eva} 1.0\linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{ccccccc} Dataset \multicolumn{2}{c}{River} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Farmland} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Hermiston} Measure OA Kappa OA Kappa OA Kappa IR-MDA 94.07 62.96 95.97 90.13 86.75 57.86 SSA-SiamNet 94.87 72. 43 94.79 87. 43 93. 93 83.19 SSCNN-S 95. 53 75.67 95.49 88. 85 95.69 87.08 CDFormer 94.92 72.76 97.34 93.58 93.06 81.57 SSTFormer 96.72 81.63 98.01 95.14 93.39 82.73 CSDBF 95.56 75.61 98.23 95.75 96.13 89.51 GTMSiam 97.11 82.93 97.37 94.03 95.66 87.26 \rowcolor{Bg} GLAFormer 97.81 83.72 98.95 97.14 97.23 91.68 {\color[HTML]{FF0000} ↑0.70\%} {\color[HTML]{FF0000} ↑0.78\%} {\color[HTML]{FF0000} ↑1.58\%} {\color[HTML]{FF0000} ↑3.11\%} {\color[HTML]{FF0000} ↑1.57\%} {\color[HTML]{FF0000} ↑4.42\%} \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-097: As presented in Table \ref{table_eva , the quantitative comparison between the GLAFormer and other methods is conducted on three datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The results demonstrate that our proposed method consistently outperforms the compared methods across all three datasets in terms of OA and Kappa.
elements.sent-2411-099: Notably, in terms of the Kappa coefficient, GLAFormer achieves an average improvement of 2.77\% across the three datasets compared to the previous state-of-the-art feature fusion method, GTMSiam.
elements.sent-2411-100: This signifies an accuracy boost of over 20\% in regions that were challenging for previous models to identify.
elements.sent-2411-101: To demonstrate the superior performance of our proposed GLAFormer, we also qualitatively compare the results of different methods on the three datasets.
elements.sent-2411-102: Fig. \ref{fig_river shows the change detection result on the River dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Unlike other methods, the IR-MAD approach exhibits significant noise due to its lack of training information in change detection.
elements.sent-2411-104: In contrast, the detection results of GLAFormer are closer to the ground truth, particularly in the highlighted regions.
elements.sent-2411-105: The change detection results of the Farmland dataset is shown in Fig. \ref{fig_farm .}
elements.sent-2411-106: The regions in the red rectangle reveal that, only the GLAFormer and SSTFormer successfully identify the subtle alterations within the region.
elements.sent-2411-107: This observation aligns with the quantitative findings reported in Table \ref{table_eva .}
elements.sent-2411-108: It is worth noting that GLAFormer demonstrates an improvement of 1.58\% and 3.11\% in OA and Kappa coefficient, respectively, compared to the GTMSiam method.
elements.sent-2411-109: This performance gain can be attributed to the superior integration of global and local signals in GLAFormer.
elements.sent-2411-110: The change detection results of Hermiston dataset are depicted in Fig. \ref{fig_sa .}
elements.sent-2411-111: It shows the complex nature of the changes within this dataset, characterized by numerous irregular regions.
elements.sent-2411-112: A detailed analysis of the highlighted regions reveals that our proposed method produces a more accurate change map with clearer boundaries and reduced noise, underscoring its robust capability in detecting changes within complex backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-113: This performance improvement can be attributed to the effectiveness of the dual-gated mechanism of CGFN in noise suppression.
elements.sent-2411-114: Quantitatively, GLAFormer achieves a 1.57\% and 4.42\% increase in OA and Kappa coefficient, respectively, compared to the GTMSiam method.
elements.sent-2411-115: Both the qualitative and quantitative analysis demonstrates the robustness and accuracy of the proposed GLAFormer in handling complex change detection scenarios.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=3.5in]{img_farm.pdf} \caption{Change detection results of different methods on Farmland dataset. (a) IR-MAD. (b) SSA-SiamNet. (c) SSCNN-S. (d) CDFomer. (e) SSTFormer. (f) CSDBF. (g) Proposed GLAFormer. (h) Ground truth.} \label{fig_farm}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=3.5in]{img_sa.pdf} \caption{Change detection results of different methods on Hermiston dataset. (a) IR-MAD. (b) SSA-SiamNet. (c) SSCNN-S. (d) CDFomer. (e) SSTFormer. (f) CSDBF. (g) Proposed GLAFormer. (h) Ground truth.} \label{fig_sa}
elements.sent-2411-116: We conduct a series of ablation experiments on the three datasets to validate the effectiveness of the proposed GLAM and CGFN.
elements.sent-2411-117: Firstly, we design a Basic Transformer with the same structure as the GLAFormer, while the Basic Transformer uses traditional multi-head attention.
elements.sent-2411-118: In addition, we have two variants of GLAFormer, i.e., without the GLAM (w/o GLAM) and without the CGFN (w/o CGFN).
elements.sent-2411-119: These are replaced with standard self-attention and FFN, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-120: The results of the ablation study are shown in Table \ref{table_ablation .}
elements.sent-2411-121: We find that GLAFormer and its variants beat the Basic Transformer in all cases.
elements.sent-2411-122: The GLAFormer always achieves better performance than its two variants on the three datasets.
elements.sent-2411-123: This demonstrates the necessity of the GLAM and CGFN designed in GLAFormer.
elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{Ablation Studies of the Proposed GLAFormer. The best performer is marked in \colorbox{Bg}{Blue}.} \label{table_ablation} \begin{tabular}{c|c c c} \multirow{2}{*}{Method} \multicolumn{3}{c}{OA on different datasets ($\%$)} River Farmland Hermiston Basic Transformer 97.19 97.87 94.42 GLAFormer w/o GLAM 97.17 98.54 95.20 GLAFormer w/o CGFN 97.64 98.66 96.97 \rowcolor{Bg} Proposed GLAFormer 97.81 98.95 97.23 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-124: In this letter, we propose a novel GLAFormer for HSI change detection.
elements.sent-2411-125: The GLAFormer offers two enhancements over existing Transformer-based change detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-126: First, the designed GLAM leverages the abundant channel information intrinsic to hyperspectral images to combine both high-frequency and low-frequency signals.
elements.sent-2411-127: Furthermore, the designed CGFN is meticulously engineered to augment the extraction of pertinent information while concurrently mitigating noise interference, thereby enhancing the overall quality of the change detection process.
elements.sent-2411-128: Our comprehensive experiments conducted on three hyperspectral datasets, consistently demonstrate the superior performance of GLAFormer over the state-of-the-art methods.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
